Background

Policy, environment,
and the tools that connect them

My double major at Tulane gave me a rare combination: rigorous policy and political analysis alongside deep environmental science. I studied abroad at the University of Auckland, expanding my perspective on how different societies approach global environmental challenges.

I've taken that foundation into real workplaces — sustainability consulting, legal research on environmental harm, and community-level environmental work. Then I learned to use AI to do all of it faster and smarter.

AI fluency

Using AI where it matters

✓ AI Literacy Badge ✓ AI Tools & Knowledge — Tulane University ✓ Digital Civic Engagement — Tulane University

I’ve taken two courses at Tulane that put AI and digital tools directly in service of real communities. In AI Tools and Knowledge, a hands-on course covering text generation, data analysis, multimodal AI, and real-world problem solving, I gained practical experience across the full AI toolkit — from external research and information analysis to working with code. I applied those skills to synthesize complex environmental policy around Louisiana’s coastal crisis, translating dense academic and government literature on land loss, sediment diversion, and sea level rise into clear, actionable insights. In a city where a football field of coastline disappears every 100 minutes, that work has real stakes.

In Digital Civic Engagement, I completed 40 hours of service to a New Orleans nonprofit, applying digital skills to build community, amplify underrepresented voices, and strengthen the organization’s online platform. The course pushed me to think about how technology can reduce inequity and expand access — not just optimize for engagement.

That same semester, I built SnapBit — a fully functional social habit-tracking app — in Google AI Studio within my first two weeks of class. No engineering background, no team. Just a clear problem, the right tools, and the initiative to build something real.

Whether it’s researching coastal policy, serving nonprofits, or building consumer apps, AI is now a core part of how I work — and I’m only getting started.

hours of digital service to a New Orleans nonprofit

Featured project

An app I built with AI

I didn’t just study AI tools — I used them to build something real. Here’s a product I conceived and created using Google AI Studio, within the first two weeks of my AI Tools class.

SnapBit

BeReal, but for the habits that change your life

After completing 75 Hard in the summer of 2025, I wanted an app that captured what that challenge felt like — the daily accountability, the photo proof, the community of people pushing each other. So within the first two weeks of my AI Tools class at Tulane, I built it. SnapBit is a social habit-tracking app where you set a goal, snap a photo every day as proof, and stay accountable with friends no matter where they are in the world. AI monitors every comment to block negative or discouraging language, keeping the community purely supportive. And when you submit a photo, AI analyzes it against your stated goal — if it doesn’t align, it gets rejected. No cheating, no shortcuts.

View live app →

The idea

Born from completing 75 Hard in summer 2025 and built within the first two weeks of my AI Tools class — SnapBit proves you don’t need a CS degree to build real products with AI.

Key features

Custom goal durations (1–365 days) · Daily photo check-ins · Buddy system · Community feed · Vision board · AI Premium Coach · Streak tracking

Built with

Built entirely in Google AI Studio — no prior coding experience, no engineering team. Just a clear vision, the right AI tools, and two weeks of work.

AI integrity layer

AI monitors every comment to block negativity and keep the community purely supportive. Photo submissions are AI-verified against your goal — if they don’t align, they get rejected. No cheating, no shortcuts.

Academic writing

Political science in practice

My Political Science major isn’t just theoretical — it shows up in how I analyze systems, form arguments, and communicate complex ideas clearly.

Crime, Policing & Criminal Courts  ·  POLA 3011  ·  Spring 2025

Luigi Mangione and the Death Penalty

A 14-page legal and political analysis examining the federal case against Luigi Mangione, the social commentary surrounding it, and the role of corporate power and healthcare policy in shaping public opinion on justice.

Analyzed all four federal charges — murder, firearms offense, and two counts of stalking — against the legal standard of proof beyond reasonable doubt
Examined jury psychology, Gen Z’s role in shaping public narrative, and the political dynamics of the Trump administration’s pursuit of the death penalty
Drew on sources from the DOJ, Pew Research Center, KFF, Harvard Medical School, and major news outlets to build an evidence-based argument
Argued that the case’s high profile reflects systemic inequities in how the justice system responds to victims based on class and corporate power
Criminal law Death penalty Healthcare policy Jury psychology Corporate power
Download full paper (PDF)

“America is the only high-income country without a universal healthcare system. Many American citizens have suffered and lost their lives at the hands of healthcare companies and their inaction, and there is an urgent need for reform.”

— Alexa Valenzuela, final paper conclusion
Professor Scott N. Nolan, Ph.D.
Course Crime, Policing & Criminal Courts

Actions, not words

Sustainability in Action

Environmental work isn’t just a career path for me — it’s something I show up for. I was taught from an early age that actions matter more than words, and that’s shaped everything I do.

Coalition to Restore Coastal Louisiana (CRCL)
Louisiana Gulf Coast

Restoring the Louisiana coastline, one oyster shell at a time

Joined a coalition of volunteers to physically restore Louisiana’s rapidly eroding coastline. We collected discarded oyster shells from seafood restaurants across New Orleans and Baton Rouge — shells that would otherwise go to landfill — and used them to rebuild reef structures that protect the shoreline and restore marine habitat.

Louisiana loses a football field of coastal land every 100 minutes. This work made that statistic real and personal. Studying coastal policy in the classroom and then standing on that coast with a shovel in your hand are two very different things — and both matter.

Coastal restoration Oyster reef rebuilding Community volunteering Environmental conservation
Recirculating Farms
New Orleans, LA  ·  Jan–May 2023

Growing food justice in New Orleans

Volunteered for a full semester at this nonprofit urban farm tackling food insecurity and food deserts across New Orleans. Cultivated and maintained fresh produce distributed directly to low-income community members who lack access to affordable, nutritious food — a critical issue in a city where food deserts are widespread.

Helped construct a tool shed and fabricate new wheelbarrows, strengthening the farm’s capacity to grow and serve more of the community.

Food justice Urban farming Food desert advocacy Community sustainability

Get to know me

Behind the resume

I’m a California girl who traded the Pacific for the Gulf Coast — and somewhere between LA, New Orleans, and Auckland, figured out exactly what I’m about.

Home

Raised in Los Angeles

Growing up in Southern California gave me a love of sunshine, spontaneity, and big-city energy. LA shaped how I move through the world — curious, driven, and never fully comfortable standing still.

School

New Orleans, Louisiana

Tulane University dropped me into one of the most complex, beautiful, and culturally rich cities in America. Studying environmental policy here — in a city literally sinking into the sea — made everything feel urgent and real. The St. Charles streetcar, the live oaks of Audubon Park, the French Quarter at midnight — this city got into my bones.

Sidequests

Always saying yes

Climbing trees in Audubon Park, bungee jumping off a bridge over the Auckland harbour, hugging a 1,000-year-old kauri tree in a New Zealand rainforest — I collect experiences the way other people collect souvenirs. Life’s too short to be a spectator.

New Orleans

Adopted hometown

Four years in and New Orleans still surprises me — from the grandeur of Jackson Square to the magic of Mardi Gras to the Mississippi at golden hour. Studying environmental policy in a city literally sinking into the sea made everything feel urgent and real.

Study abroad

A semester in New Zealand

Spending five months at the University of Auckland was one of the best decisions I’ve ever made. From hiking to Cape Reinga at the very tip of the North Island to sailing the Bay of Islands, New Zealand rewired how I think about environmental stewardship — and reminded me that the world is much bigger than any one city.

Fur babies

Bendo & Judith

My two Great Pyrenees — Bendo is 6 and Judith is 3 — and I love them with my whole heart. They come everywhere, they hog the bed, and I wouldn’t have it any other way.

Let's talk sustainability.

Graduating May 2026. Actively exploring entry-level roles in Dallas and Houston with companies committed to real environmental impact. Bilingual in English and Spanish.
